summary:
Pastoral nomadism — a way of life organized around the seasonal movement of herds — began to emerge on the Pontic-Caspian steppe during the fifth millennium BC as communities developed the capacity to exploit the grasslands away from permanent water sources. The gradual domestication of horses for riding, associated with cultures such as the [[event:1541|Botai culture]] of Kazakhstan and the [[event:1538|Pontic-Caspian steppe cultures]], enabled herders to manage larger flocks over greater distances than was possible on foot. The introduction of wheeled vehicles in the same period further enhanced mobility.

Early pastoral nomadism on the steppe was not a complete abandonment of settlement but rather a flexible strategy that combined seasonal movement of animals with base camps or winter settlements. These communities developed a distinctive material culture adapted to mobile life — including portable shelters, lightweight ceramics, and horse equipment — that would evolve over subsequent millennia into the fully nomadic traditions of the Bronze and Iron Age steppe. The spread of pastoral peoples across the Eurasian grasslands had profound demographic and linguistic consequences, contributing to the dispersal of [[event:1544|Proto-Indo-European]] languages across a vast geographic range.
